titleStmt.title: The Secretary of State to the Consul General at Shanghai ( Cabot )

div.head: The Secretary of State to the Consul General at Shanghai ( Cabot )
div.p:
336. For Cabot and Parker from State & Treas. State and Treas do not consider that present conditions in China warrant grant or loan to China for purposes outlined urtel 550. For your background information
only considerations which led to Secy Marshall’sGeneral of the Army George C. Marshall, Secretary of State, January 1947–January 1949. expression of views on use of silver before House Foreign Affairs Committee on Feb 20, 1948, United States Foreign Policy for a Post-War Recovery Program: Hearings, 80th Cong., 2nd sess. (Washington, Government Printing Office, 1948), pt. 2, p. 1569. expanded statement subsequently submitted to committeeEntitled Possible Use of Silver for Monetary Stabilization in China in Connection With the China Aid Program; for text, see Department of State, United States Relations With China (Washington, Government Printing Office, 1949), p. 987. and airpouched Emb Depins 165 Aug 13, 1948 to NankingNot printed. and Secy Snyder’sJohn W. Snyder, Secretary of the Treasury. views expressed in letter Aug 30, 1947 to Secy Marshall
Foreign Relations, 1947, vol. vii, p. 1182. transmitted to Emb as Airgram No. 138 of Sept 9, 1947Not printed. appear to be equally applicable even to limited operation of type outlined reftel. No encouragement should be given to Chinese Govt silver proposal. [State and Treasury.]
